question_title: What will be the value of "Market Yield on U.S. Treasury Securities at 1-Year Constant Maturity, Quoted on an Investment Basis" on 2026-05-14?
summary: The 1-year US Treasury yield (DGS1) on May 14, 2026 is forecast to remain near its current value of 3.72%. The Fed held rates unchanged at 3.5-3.75% on April 29 with no FOMC meeting until June 17, anchoring the 1-year yield near the top of the fed funds band. The yield has been in a stable regime (3.64-3.75%) for 19 trading days with daily volatility of ~2.5 bps. A Monte Carlo simulation (500K paths, 70/30 mixture of base and elevated volatility to account for geopolitical tail risks from Middle East tensions) produces a distribution centered at 3.72 with a 10-day random walk standard deviation of ~9.4 bps.

factors.description: Stable regime: DGS1 has been in a tight range (3.64-3.75%) for 19 trading days with mean 3.694 and std 0.024. No structural break detected.
Fed held rates unchanged at 3.5-3.75% on Apr 29 with no rate change expected before May 14. Next FOMC is June 17. 1-year yield anchored near top of fed funds band.
Most recent value 3.72% - slight uptick in last 2 days (from 3.69 to 3.75 to 3.72). No clear drift direction.
Middle East uncertainty (Iran blockade) mentioned in FOMC statement as source of elevated uncertainty - could cause wider-than-normal swings in risk sentiment.
FOMC dissent (4 members, most since 1992) signals policy uncertainty - some want cuts, others oppose easing bias. This creates two-sided risk for short-term yields.

reasoning:
**Forecast Summary: DGS1 on May 14, 2026**

| Percentile | Value |
|---|---|
| P1 | 3.49% |
| P5 | 3.57% |
| P10 | 3.60% |
| P25 | 3.66% |
| P50 | 3.72% |
| P75 | 3.78% |
| P90 | 3.84% |
| P95 | 3.87% |
| P99 | 3.95% |

**Key reasoning:**
- **Center at 3.72%** — the current value, with essentially zero drift over the stable regime
- **Tight distribution** — 80% CI of [3.60, 3.84] reflecting the low volatility environment (~2.5 bps/day) with no FOMC meeting before resolution
- **Fed anchoring** — rates held at 3.5-3.75% with next meeting not until June 17; the 1-year yield is tethered near the top of the band
- **Fat tails from 70/30 volatility mixture** — accounts for potential geopolitical surprises (Middle East tensions) or unexpected economic data that could push yields outside the recent 3.64-3.75 range

resolution_criteria: Resolves to the value found on the FRED API for the series DGS1 once the data is published.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will paginate through the data found at the API endpoint `https://api.stlouisfed.org/fred/series/observations?series_id=DGS1`. This endpoint includes the latest data for the series. The latest revised data will be used when the resolution is assessed. The datapoint matching 2026-05-14 will be used to determine the resolution of this question.

A datapoint matches if:
1. The series is updated daily and the date of the datapoint is within 1 day previous to the resolution date.
2. The series is updated weekly and the date of the datapoint is within 7 days previous to the resolution date.
3. The series is updated monthly and the date of the datapoint is within 31 days previous to the resolution date.

If the datapoint is clearly skipped, or no datapoint is found after 2 weeks of checking then the question will be annulled.

factors.description: Stable regime: DGS1 has been in a tight range (3.64-3.75%) for 19 trading days with mean 3.694 and std 0.024. No structural break detected.
Fed held rates unchanged at 3.5-3.75% on Apr 29 with no rate change expected before May 14. Next FOMC is June 17. 1-year yield anchored near top of fed funds band.
Most recent value 3.72% - slight uptick in last 2 days (from 3.69 to 3.75 to 3.72). No clear drift direction.
Middle East uncertainty (Iran blockade) mentioned in FOMC statement as source of elevated uncertainty - could cause wider-than-normal swings in risk sentiment.
FOMC dissent (4 members, most since 1992) signals policy uncertainty - some want cuts, others oppose easing bias. This creates two-sided risk for short-term yields.

input.anchor: Current value: 3.72%. Regime mean: 3.694% (19 observations). Random walk from 3.72 with daily vol 0.025.
input.assessment: The 1-year Treasury yield is tightly anchored near 3.72%, close to the top of the fed funds target range (3.5-3.75%). With no FOMC meeting until June 17 and rates on hold for a third consecutive meeting, the primary driver of 1-year yields (monetary policy expectations) is stable. The empirical daily volatility is ~2.5 bps, giving a random-walk 10-day standard deviation of ~8 bps. The Monte Carlo simulation (70/30 mixture of base and elevated volatility) produces a distribution centered at 3.72 with P10=3.60 and P90=3.84. The Middle East tensions and FOMC dissent add some tail risk but don't shift the center.
input.calibration_notes: Distribution centered on current value (3.72) with no meaningful drift. Width driven by empirical daily volatility scaled to 10 trading days. 70/30 mixture adds modest fat tails for geopolitical/macro surprise scenarios. P10-P90 range of 24 bps seems appropriate for a 2-week horizon in a stable rate environment.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether Middle East tensions escalate enough to cause a flight-to-safety rally (pushing yields down) or inflation scare (pushing up). 2. Whether any unexpected economic data (jobs, CPI) between now and May 14 shifts rate expectations significantly. 3. The 19-day calibration window may not capture rare large moves.
input.tool_audit: FRED DGS1 data via web_search: excellent - returned full 19-day time series with regime stats. FOMC statement fetch: very useful - confirmed rate hold, dissent details, and policy stance. TradingEconomics: useful for confirming market expectations. No tool failures.
input.update_triggers: Major geopolitical escalation in Middle East; surprise CPI or jobs data; unexpected Fed communication shifting rate expectations; market liquidity event.
input.process_reflection: This was a straightforward short-horizon yield forecast. The FRED data integration in web_search was excellent, providing structured JSON with regime stats that made Monte Carlo parameterization easy. The main challenge was deciding on the right volatility mixture - the 19-day window is short but the regime is clearly stable. I used a 70/30 base/high-vol mixture to account for potential surprises. The forecasting framework worked well for this type of measurement question.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Checks Performed

### Hallucinated Evidence — PASS
All five factors are grounded in the research trace:

- **Factor 1 (Stable regime)**: Confirmed by FRED API regime_stats: stable_mean=3.693684, stable_std=0.024107, observations_in_regime=19, stable_since 2026-04-06. Data range 3.64-3.75 matches.
- **Factor 2 (Fed held rates, next FOMC June 17)**: FOMC statement fetch confirms target range 3.5-3.75%, held unchanged. TradingEconomics confirms "third consecutive meeting." Investing.com snippet confirms "Fed Interest Rate Decision Jun 17, 2026."
- **Factor 3 (Most recent 3.72%, slight uptick)**: FRED data shows Apr 27=3.69, Apr 28=3.71, Apr 29=3.75, Apr 30=3.72. Factor says "from 3.69 to 3.75 to 3.72" — minor simplification skipping 3.71 but core claim is accurate.
- **Factor 4 (Middle East/Iran blockade in FOMC statement)**: FOMC fetch confirms "elevated inflation partly due to increased global energy prices and uncertainty from Middle East developments." TradingEconomics: "pledged to maintain the naval blockade."
- **Factor 5 (4 dissents, most since 1992)**: Confirmed by multiple sources — FOMC statement, Schwab, TradingEconomics all confirm 4 dissents and the "most since 1992" characterization. Dissent descriptions (Miran wanted cut; Hammack/Kashkari/Logan opposed easing bias) match the trace.

### Double-Counting — PASS
Factors 4 and 5 both derive from the FOMC meeting context, but describe independent phenomena (geopolitical tail risk vs. policy disagreement). Both have small logits (+0.3, +0.2) and low confidence (0.5), so overlap impact is negligible.

### Wrong-Direction Factors — PASS
All logit signs are consistent with their descriptions. Positive logits support the stated directional pulls.

### Contradictory Assessment — PASS
Assessment centers distribution at 3.72 with P10=3.60, P90=3.84, citing stable regime with tail risk. This matches the factor balance perfectly — anchored near current value, small risk factors in both directions.

### Resolution Criteria — PASS
Criteria clearly specify FRED API series DGS1, data point for 2026-05-14. The agent correctly targets this date and series.

### Regime-Spanning Data Window — PASS
19-day window (Apr 6-30) shows no structural break per regime_stats. The April 29 FOMC spike (3.75) and quick reversion (3.72 next day) suggest no regime change. The agent appropriately used a 70/30 base/elevated volatility mixture model to account for potential under-estimation from the calm window.

### Counterargument Quality — PASS
The counterargument identifies specific, quantified scenarios: Middle East escalation (15-25 bps), surprise jobs report, Fed official speech (10-15 bps), CPI acceleration (10-20 bps). It also critiques the 19-day calibration window as potentially underestimating tail risk. This is substantive and constructs a coherent alternative story.

### Confidence Self-Assessment — PASS
0.65 is appropriate for a 10-day Treasury yield forecast with stable regime data but geopolitical tail risks and the inherent difficulty of short-term yield prediction.

### Probability Assessment — PASS
Center at 3.72 (current value) with 80% CI [3.60, 3.84] is well-calibrated. Random walk: daily vol 0.025 × √10 ≈ 0.079, so ±1.5σ gives [3.60, 3.84], consistent with the mixture model output. The Monte Carlo simulation (500K paths, 70/30 mixture) producing mean=3.72, std=0.094 is mechanically sound.

### Anchor Divergence — PASS
Anchor (3.72) = center estimate (3.72). No divergence.

### Tool Audit — PASS
Agent reports "38 calls, 0 errors" and "No tool failures." Ground truth confirms: 38 calls, 0 errors, 0.0% error rate. No discrepancy.

### Worldview Consistency — PASS
Worldview archive entry (Federal Reserve policy, April 15) confirms rates at 3.50-3.75%, markets pricing no changes through July 2026. Consistent with Factor 2. The "Iran war" factor in the worldview entry is consistent with Factor 4. No contradictions.

## Summary
This is a well-executed numeric forecast. All five factors are grounded in the research trace. The Monte Carlo simulation methodology is sound (mixture model with base and elevated volatility). The distribution is mechanically derived from verified data with appropriate uncertainty bands. No errors found.
